title: St. Samuel the Prophet — St. Cyril of Alexandria (Monthly) & St. Columba of Iona
primary_saint: St. Samuel the Prophet and Judge of Israel, the Monthly Commemoration of St. Cyril the Great Archbishop of Alexandria the Pillar of Faith, and St. Columba of Iona the Apostle of Scotland
narrative:
On the ninth of Sene, the holy Church celebrates the feast of the holy Prophet Samuel, the last of the Judges and the first of the writing prophets, the man who anointed both Saul and David as kings of Israel and who in so doing stands at the hinge of the Hebrew political theology, presiding over the transition from the era of the judges to the era of the monarchy that the tradition reads as a prefiguration of the transition from the Law to the Gospel. Samuel was dedicated to the Lord before his birth by his mother Hannah — the woman whose prayer of thanksgiving at Samuel's birth became the model for the Magnificat, the song of another mother at the birth of another son whose coming would change the world — and his life from its beginning to its end is the life of one who belongs entirely to God: given to the Temple as a child, called by God in the night while he slept near the ark, anointed as prophet and judge, responsible for the anointing of the kings who would rule Israel.

The theology of Samuel in the Tewahedo tradition is a theology of the prophetic anointing: Samuel is the one who anoints, who pours the holy oil on the heads of those whom God has chosen, and in this function he prefigures every subsequent anointing in the tradition — the anointing of priests and kings, the anointing of the sick, the anointing of the baptised with chrism, all of which are participations in the anointing of Christ himself, the Messiah whose very name means the Anointed One. The tradition also honours Samuel for the extraordinary faithfulness of his own prophetic vocation: he who told Saul to his face that the kingdom had been taken from him because of his disobedience, he who mourned Saul's fall with a grief that was genuine even as he announced its inevitability, he who anointed the young David in the house of Jesse when David was still tending sheep — Samuel is the man who served the divine word without flinching even when the divine word brought pain to the one who carried it.

Saint Cyril the Great of Alexandria receives his monthly commemoration on this ninth day of Sene as the great theological warrior of the fifth century whose combat against the Nestorian heresy at the Council of Ephesus in four hundred and thirty-one secured for all time the Church's confession that the Virgin Mary is rightly called Theotokos — the God-bearer, the Mother of God — and that the one born of her is not a man assumed by God but God himself assuming human nature. Cyril is a figure of intense controversy in the historical record, a man whose methods were sometimes as brutal as his theology was precise, and the tradition does not minimise the complexity: it honours him for what he preserved rather than for how he preserved it, for the doctrinal clarity that the Council of Ephesus achieved rather than for all of the actions by which Cyril prosecuted his case. The monthly commemoration of Cyril places his theological achievement in the context of the perpetual celebration: the Church that gives thanks for Cyril each month is a Church that uses the Theotokos in every liturgy, that prays to the Mother of God in every hour of prayer, that benefits continuously from the doctrinal clarity he fought to establish.

Saint Columba of Iona, the great Irish monk and missionary of the sixth century, brings to this day the witness of the Celtic Christian tradition: a man of noble Irish birth who became a monk, who founded monasteries in Ireland, and who eventually left Ireland in a voluntary exile — an exile chosen as a form of ascetic offering, the peregrinatio pro Christo that was one of the distinctive vocations of the Celtic monastic tradition — and settled on the island of Iona off the west coast of Scotland. From Iona, Columba and his community evangelised the Picts of Scotland and from Scotland the faith spread further into the Anglo-Saxon kingdoms of Britain. Columba is the tradition's icon of the missionary monk: the man who understood that the monastery is not only a place of withdrawal but a base of mission, a centre from which the light of the Gospel radiates outward into the surrounding darkness, and that the monk's prayer and the missionary's journey are not two activities but one, the inward and the outward dimensions of the single response to Christ's commission to make disciples of all nations. The ninth day of Sene thus gathers the prophet who anointed Israel's kings, the doctor who defined the Mother of God, and the monk who carried the faith to the ends of the known world. Amen.
